title: 𝗟𝗼𝗰𝗮𝗹𝗶𝘀𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 𝗶𝗻 𝗦𝗼𝗳𝘁𝘄𝗮𝗿𝗲 𝗗𝗲𝘃𝗲𝗹𝗼𝗽𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁 (Frontend Developers)
content:
A startup built a product for 6 months. Launched in Japan. Zero users. App was fully in English. 🤦 Here's what Localisation actually means — and how to do it right 👇 --- 🧠 **𝗪𝗵𝗮𝘁 𝗶𝘀 𝗟𝗼𝗰𝗮𝗹𝗶𝘀𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻?** 👉 **i18n** (Internationalisation) — designing app to support multiple languages 👉 **L10n** (Localisation) — actually adapting it for a specific region 👉 They are NOT the same thing ```plaintext i18n = building the infrastructure L10n = filling it with regional content i18n is done once. L10n is done for every new market. ``` ✔ Language translation ✔ Date, time & number formats ✔ Currency symbols ✔ Text direction (LTR vs RTL) ✔ Cultural sensitivities --- 📝 1️⃣ **𝗧𝗿𝗮𝗻𝘀𝗹𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 — 𝗡𝗲𝘃𝗲𝗿 𝗛𝗮𝗿𝗱𝗰𝗼𝗱𝗲 𝗦𝘁𝗿𝗶𝗻𝗴𝘀** 👉 Every visible string must live outside your code ```js // ❌ Hardcoded — impossible to translate

Welcome back, John!

// ✅ Using i18n keys

{t('welcome', { name: 'John' })}

// en.json { "welcome": "Welcome back, {{name}}!" } // ja.json { "welcome": "おかえりなさい、{{name}}さん！" } ``` ✔ Use libraries: `react-i18next`, `i18next`, `react-intl` ✔ Keys should be semantic — not the English text itself ✔ Never use Google Translate for production — hire translators --- 📅 2️⃣ **𝗗𝗮𝘁𝗲 & 𝗧𝗶𝗺𝗲 𝗙𝗼𝗿𝗺𝗮𝘁𝘀** 👉 Date formats are wildly different across regions ```js // ❌ Hardcoded format — confusing globally "04/05/2025" // Is this April 5 or May 4? // ✅ Use Intl.DateTimeFormat new Intl.DateTimeFormat('en-US').format(date) // → "4/5/2025" new Intl.DateTimeFormat('en-GB').format(date) // → "05/04/2025" new Intl.DateTimeFormat('ja-JP').format(date) // → "2025/4/5" ``` ✔ Always store dates in UTC internally ✔ Format only at display time ✔ Never hardcode date separators or order --- 💰 3️⃣ **𝗖𝘂𝗿𝗿𝗲𝗻𝗰𝘆 & 𝗡𝘂𝗺𝗯𝗲𝗿𝘀** 👉 Numbers & currency look different in every country ```js // ❌ Hardcoded — wrong for most of the world "$1,000.50" // ✅ Use Intl.NumberFormat new Intl.NumberFormat('en-US', { style: 'currency', currency: 'USD' }).format(1000.50) // → "$1,000.50" new Intl.NumberFormat('de-DE', { style: 'currency', currency: 'EUR' }).format(1000.50) // → "1.000,50 €" ``` ✔ Decimal separator: `.` in US, `,` in Germany ✔ Currency symbol position varies by locale ✔ Always use `Intl.NumberFormat` — never manual formatting --- ↔️ 4️⃣ **𝗥𝗧𝗟 𝗟𝗮𝗻𝗴𝘂𝗮𝗴𝗲𝘀 (𝗥𝗶𝗴𝗵𝘁-𝘁𝗼-𝗟𝗲𝗳𝘁)** 👉 Arabic, Hebrew, Urdu — entire layout flips direction ```html
Content
Content
Icon
``` ✔ Use `dir="rtl"` on `` tag ✔ CSS logical properties: `margin-inline-start` instead of `margin-left` ✔ Test your entire layout in RTL — icons, buttons, animations all flip --- 🕐 5️⃣ **𝗧𝗶𝗺𝗲𝘇𝗼𝗻𝗲𝘀** 👉 One of the most common bugs in global apps ```js // ❌ Using local time — breaks for other timezones new Date().toString() // ✅ Always work in UTC, format locally new Intl.DateTimeFormat('en-US', { timeZone: 'Asia/Tokyo', hour: '2-digit', minute: '2-digit' }).format(new Date()) // ✅ Use date-fns-tz or Luxon for complex timezone logic import { zonedTimeToUtc } from 'date-fns-tz'; ``` ✔ Store all timestamps in UTC in your database ✔ Convert to user's timezone only at display ✔ Never use `new Date()` for timezone-sensitive features --- 🔤 6️⃣ **𝗣𝗹𝘂𝗿𝗮𝗹𝗶𝘀𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 & 𝗚𝗲𝗻𝗱𝗲𝗿** 👉 "1 item" vs "2 items" — every language handles this differently ```js // ❌ English-only logic — fails in Russian, Arabic `You have ${count} message${count !== 1 ? 's' : ''}` // ✅ i18next plural handling t('messages', { count: 5 }) // en.json { "messages_one": "You have {{count}} message", "messages_other": "You have {{count}} messages" } // ar.json — Arabic has 6 plural forms! { "messages_zero": "...", "messages_one": "...", "messages_two": "...", "messages_few": "...", "messages_many": "...", "messages_other": "..." } ``` ✔ Never build plural logic manually ✔ Use CLDR plural rules via i18next ✔ Arabic has 6 forms. Russian has 3. Plan for this. --- 🖼️ 7️⃣ **𝗜𝗺𝗮𝗴𝗲𝘀, 𝗜𝗰𝗼𝗻𝘀 & 𝗖𝗼𝗹𝗼𝘂𝗿𝘀** 👉 Visuals carry cultural meaning — not just language ✔ 👍 Thumbs up = positive in West, offensive in Middle East ✔ ⚪ White = purity in West, mourning in parts of Asia ✔ Avoid country-specific idioms in illustrations ✔ Flag icons can be politically sensitive ```js // ❌ Using flags to represent languages 🇺🇸 English 🇫🇷 French // ✅ Use language names — not flags EN FR DE JA ``` --- ⚛️ **𝗥𝗲𝗮𝗹 𝗦𝗲𝘁𝘂𝗽 𝗶𝗻 𝗥𝗲𝗮𝗰𝘁** ```bash npm install react-i18next i18next ``` ```js // i18n.js import i18n from 'i18next'; import { initReactI18next } from 'react-i18next'; i18n.use(initReactI18next).init({ lng: 'en', fallbackLng: 'en', resources: { en: { translation: require('./locales/en.json') }, ja: { translation: require('./locales/ja.json') } } }); // Component usage const { t, i18n } = useTranslation();

{t('welcome')}

``` ✔ Lazy load translations — don't bundle all languages upfront ✔ Detect browser language automatically with `i18next-browser-languagedetector` ✔ Store user language preference in localStorage --- 🚨 **𝗖𝗼𝗺𝗺𝗼𝗻 𝗠𝗶𝘀𝘁𝗮𝗸𝗲𝘀** ❌ Hardcoding strings directly in components ❌ Using `new Date()` without timezone handling ❌ Building plural logic manually in English ❌ Using flag icons to represent languages ❌ Adding i18n as an afterthought — design for it from day one ❌ Assuming all text expands the same — German text is 30% longer than English --- 💡 **𝗦𝗲𝗻𝗶𝗼𝗿-𝗟𝗲𝘃𝗲𝗹 𝗜𝗻𝘀𝗶𝗴𝗵𝘁** i18n is architecture. L10n is content. Confuse the two and you rebuild from scratch. Design for localisation on day one — retrofitting it costs 10x more. --- 🎯 **𝗜𝗻𝘁𝗲𝗿𝘃𝗶𝗲𝘄 𝗢𝗻𝗲-𝗟𝗶𝗻𝗲𝗿** Localisation (L10n) is the process of adapting software for a specific region — covering language translation, date/time/currency formatting, RTL support, pluralisation, and cultural nuances — built on top of Internationalisation (i18n), which is the architecture that makes localisation possible without rewriting code. --- **Is your current app localised? Which language did you add first? 👇** `#Localisation` `#i18n` `#Frontend` `#ReactJS` `#WebDevelopment` `#JavaScript` `#InterviewPrep` `#GlobalProduct` `#EngineeringMindset`
